## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ontario&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Education")]

Main article: [Education in Ontario](/wiki/Education_in_Ontario "Education in Ontario")

Ontario operates four publicly funded school systems, with there being both English and French equivalents of the public school system and the Catholic school system.[[110]](#cite_note-116) About half of Ontario's government-funded District School Boards are Catholic (37 out of 72).[[111]](#cite_note-117) There are some publicly funded schools with non-Catholic religious affiliation: these include [Eden High School](/wiki/Eden_High_School "Eden High School") (under the [District School Board of Niagara](/wiki/District_School_Board_of_Niagara "District School Board of Niagara"))[[112]](#cite_note-118) and the Burkevale Protestant Separate School (under the [Penetanguishene Protestant Separate School Board](/wiki/Penetanguishene_Protestant_Separate_School_Board "Penetanguishene Protestant Separate School Board")).[[113]](#cite_note-119) Legislation regarding primary and secondary level education in Ontario is outlined in the *Education Act*.[[114]](#cite_note-120) In 2021, two million children were enrolled as students within the province.[[115]](#cite_note-121) [Elementary schools](/wiki/Elementary_school "Elementary school") teach children enrolled in kindergarten and grades 1–8, while [secondary schools](/wiki/Secondary_school "Secondary school") teach adolescents in grades 9–12. Four and five year olds may enter [kindergarten](/wiki/Kindergarten "Kindergarten") programs but are not required by law to do so until they turn six that calendar year.[[116]](#cite_note-122)

### Higher education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ontario&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Higher education")]

Main article: [Higher education in Ontario](/wiki/Higher_education_in_Ontario "Higher education in Ontario")

See also: [List of colleges in Ontario](/wiki/List_of_colleges_in_Ontario "List of colleges in Ontario") and [List of universities in Ontario](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Ontario "List of universities in Ontario")

Higher education in Ontario includes postsecondary education and skills training regulated by the [Ministry of Colleges and Universities](/wiki/Ministry_of_Colleges_and_Universities "Ministry of Colleges and Universities"), whose current minister is [Jill Dunlop](/wiki/Jill_Dunlop "Jill Dunlop").[[117]](#cite_note-123) Recognized institutions include universities, colleges of applied arts and technology, and private career colleges.[[118]](#cite_note-MTCU-Role_of_the_ministry-124) While there is some overlap between the purpose of universities and colleges in Canada, they generally serve different purposes. Universities place greater emphasis on academics while colleges place greater emphasis on [work-integrated learning](/wiki/Work-integrated_learning "Work-integrated learning"). Both colleges and universities can offer [undergraduate degree](/wiki/Undergraduate_degree "Undergraduate degree") programs.[[119]](#cite_note-125) There are also programs that involve a partnership between a college and a university. Some students choose to attend college over university because it is the more affordable option.[[120]](#cite_note-126)

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ontario&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Culture")]

See also: [Category:Works about Ontario](/wiki/Category:Works_about_Ontario "Category:Works about Ontario"), [Hollywood North](/wiki/Hollywood_North "Hollywood North"), [Southern Ontario Gothic](/wiki/Southern_Ontario_Gothic "Southern Ontario Gothic"), [Northern Ontario § Culture](/wiki/Northern_Ontario#Culture "Northern Ontario"), and [Franco-Ontarians § Culture](/wiki/Franco-Ontarians#Culture "Franco-Ontarians")

Outdoor recreation is popular in Ontario and the region is home to numerous cultural events and festivals. There is no single regional dish in Ontario. Local fish and wild game, such as [walleye](/wiki/Walleye "Walleye") and [moose](/wiki/Moose "Moose"), are sometimes consumed. Poutine, a dish that originated in Quebec, is also popular in Ontario.

In 2019, the government of Ontario passed legislation that established the [Poet Laureate of Ontario](/wiki/Poet_Laureate_of_Ontario "Poet Laureate of Ontario").[[121]](#cite_note-CBCnews-127)

### Museums

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ontario&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Museums")]

Main article: [List of museums in Ontario](/wiki/List_of_museums_in_Ontario "List of museums in Ontario")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Royal_Ontario_Museum_in_Fall_2021.jpg/250px-Royal_Ontario_Museum_in_Fall_2021.jpg)](/wiki/File:Royal_Ontario_Museum_in_Fall_2021.jpg)

Royal Ontario Museum in fall of 2021

The largest museum in both Ontario and Canada is the [Royal Ontario Museum](/wiki/Royal_Ontario_Museum "Royal Ontario Museum"), located in Toronto and founded in 1912. Receiving over one million visitors each year, it is also Canada's most popular museum.[[122]](#cite_note-128)[[123]](#cite_note-129) It features 40 exhibits containing "art, culture and nature from around the world."[[124]](#cite_note-130) Iconic objects include: the world's largest faceted [cerussite](/wiki/Cerussite "Cerussite") gem, [Light of the Desert](/wiki/Light_of_the_Desert "Light of the Desert"); four large totem poles, [Nisga'a and Haida](/wiki/Nisga%27a_and_Haida_Crest_Poles_of_the_Royal_Ontario_Museum "Nisga'a and Haida Crest Poles of the Royal Ontario Museum"); and a [Neo-Babylonian](/wiki/Neo-Babylonian "Neo-Babylonian") wall relief, [Striding Lion](/wiki/Striding_Lion "Striding Lion").[[125]](#cite_note-131)

Ontario is also home to a number of national museums, due to the location of Ottawa within Ontario. These include, among others, the [Canadian War Museum](/wiki/Canadian_War_Museum "Canadian War Museum"), dedicated to [Canada's military history](/wiki/Military_history_of_Canada "Military history of Canada"), the [Canadian Museum of Nature](/wiki/Canadian_Museum_of_Nature "Canadian Museum of Nature"), dedicated to [natural history](/wiki/Natural_history "Natural history") and the [Canada Science and Technology Museum](/wiki/Canada_Science_and_Technology_Museum "Canada Science and Technology Museum"), dedicated to the [history of science and technology in Canada](/wiki/Technological_and_industrial_history_of_Canada "Technological and industrial history of Canada").[[126]](#cite_note-132)[[127]](#cite_note-133)

There are also numerous other smaller, regional museums located in Ontario.[[128]](#cite_note-134)

### Music and arts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ontario&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Music and arts")]

See also: [Music of Ontario](/wiki/Music_of_Ontario "Music of Ontario") and [Canadian art](/wiki/Canadian_art "Canadian art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/The_Tragically_Hip_Star_on_Canada%27s_Walk_of_Fame.jpg/250px-The_Tragically_Hip_Star_on_Canada%27s_Walk_of_Fame.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Tragically_Hip_Star_on_Canada%27s_Walk_of_Fame.jpg)

[The Tragically Hip](/wiki/The_Tragically_Hip "The Tragically Hip") star on Canada's Walk of Fame in Toronto

Ontario has a particularly prominent role in [Canadian music](/wiki/Music_of_Canada "Music of Canada"),[[129]](#cite_note-135) notably Toronto, Canada's largest municipality.[[130]](#cite_note-136) In classical music, the [Toronto Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Toronto_Symphony_Orchestra "Toronto Symphony Orchestra"),[[131]](#cite_note-sym-137) and the [National Arts Centre Orchestra](/wiki/National_Arts_Centre_Orchestra "National Arts Centre Orchestra")[[132]](#cite_note-138) are renowned internationally.[[133]](#cite_note-139) Many smaller Ontario cities have orchestras of their own as well. The [Canadian Opera Company](/wiki/Canadian_Opera_Company "Canadian Opera Company"),[[134]](#cite_note-140) also based in Toronto, is the country's largest and most influential producer of opera productions. Other institutions in the province include the [Royal Conservatory of Music](/wiki/Royal_Conservatory_of_Music_(Canada) "Royal Conservatory of Music (Canada)"),[[135]](#cite_note-141) [MuchMusic](/wiki/MuchMusic "MuchMusic"),[[136]](#cite_note-142) [National Ballet of Canada](/wiki/National_Ballet_of_Canada "National Ballet of Canada")[[137]](#cite_note-143) and concert venues such as [Roy Thomson Hall](/wiki/Roy_Thomson_Hall "Roy Thomson Hall"),[[138]](#cite_note-roy-144) [Massey Hall](/wiki/Massey_Hall "Massey Hall"),[[138]](#cite_note-roy-144) the [National Arts Centre](/wiki/National_Arts_Centre_(building) "National Arts Centre (building)"),[[139]](#cite_note-145) and the [Four Seasons Centre](/wiki/Four_Seasons_Centre "Four Seasons Centre").[[140]](#cite_note-146)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ontario&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Media")]

In 2022, Ontario had 357 newspapers, 32 of which are [daily](/wiki/Daily_newspaper "Daily newspaper"), the highest in any province.[[141]](#cite_note-147) Ontario is home to the largest newspaper[[c]](#cite_note-148) in Canada, the *[Toronto Star](/wiki/Toronto_Star "Toronto Star")*,[[142]](#cite_note-149) and Canada's [newspaper of record](/wiki/Newspaper_of_record "Newspaper of record"), [The Globe and Mail](/wiki/The_Globe_and_Mail "The Globe and Mail").[[143]](#cite_note-150)[[144]](#cite_note-151)[[145]](#cite_note-152)[[146]](#cite_note-153) There are also numerous weekly newspapers for individual communities, though print publications for these papers have been on a downwards trend due to local news being shared on sites like [Facebook](/wiki/Facebook "Facebook").[[147]](#cite_note-154)[[148]](#cite_note-155)

### Songs and slogans

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ontario&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Songs and slogans")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/04/1997_Ontario_license_plate_CAJA%E2%99%94723.jpg/250px-1997_Ontario_license_plate_CAJA%E2%99%94723.jpg)](/wiki/File:1997_Ontario_license_plate_CAJA%E2%99%94723.jpg)

An [Ontario licence plate](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Ontario "Vehicle registration plates of Ontario") with the slogan *Yours to Discover* at the bottom of the plate

In 1973, the first slogan to appear on [licence plates in Ontario](/wiki/Vehicle_registration_plates_of_Ontario "Vehicle registration plates of Ontario") was "Keep It Beautiful". This was replaced by "Yours to Discover" in 1982,[[149]](#cite_note-156) which was originally used as a tourism slogan beginning in 1980.[[150]](#cite_note-157) Plates with the French equivalent, *Tant à découvrir*, were made available to the public beginning in May 2008.[[151]](#cite_note-158) (From 1988 to 1990,[[152]](#cite_note-159) "Ontario Incredible"[[153]](#cite_note-160) gave "Yours to Discover" a brief respite.) In 2020, as part of a licence plate redesign, the slogan was changed to "A Place to Grow," inspired by the song [A Place to Stand, a Place to Grow](/wiki/A_Place_to_Stand,_a_Place_to_Grow "A Place to Stand, a Place to Grow"). This decision was reversed in the same year, due to visibility concerns.[[154]](#cite_note-161)[[155]](#cite_note-162) The slogan on licence plates remains "Yours to Discover".[[156]](#cite_note-163)

A Place to Stand, a Place to Grow was originally commissioned by the government of Ontario for its pavilion in [Expo 67](/wiki/Expo_67 "Expo 67"), and it became an unofficial anthem of the province.[[157]](#cite_note-aplaceto-164) As a part of the [Canada 150](/wiki/Canada_150 "Canada 150") celebrations in 2017, the provincial government released an updated rendition.[[157]](#cite_note-aplaceto-164) In 2007, the provincial tourism agency commissioned a new song, "There's No Place Like This" is featured in [television advertising](/wiki/Television_advertisement "Television advertisement"), performed by Ontario artists including [Molly Johnson](/wiki/Molly_Johnson "Molly Johnson"), [Brian Byrne](/wiki/Brian_Byrne "Brian Byrne"), [Keshia Chanté](/wiki/Keshia_Chant%C3%A9 "Keshia Chanté"),[[158]](#cite_note-165) as well as [Tomi Swick](/wiki/Tomi_Swick "Tomi Swick") and [Arkells](/wiki/Arkells "Arkells").

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ontario&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Transportation")]

Transportation in Ontario is under the purview of the [Ministry of Transportation of Ontario](/wiki/Ministry_of_Transportation_of_Ontario "Ministry of Transportation of Ontario") and [Transport Canada](/wiki/Transport_Canada "Transport Canada"). Infrastructure and laws relating to road transport is the responsibility of the Ministry of Transportation, while infrastructure and laws relating to air, rail and marine transport is the responsibility of Transport Canada.[[159]](#cite_note-166)[[160]](#cite_note-167)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Toronto_Pearson_International_Airport_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Toronto_Pearson_International_Airport_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Toronto_Pearson_International_Airport_-_panoramio.jpg)

[Airport apron](/wiki/Airport_apron "Airport apron") of Pearson Airport, Canada's busiest airport. Control tower visible in background.

There are two Transport Canada designated [international airports in Ontario](/wiki/List_of_international_airports_in_Canada "List of international airports in Canada")[[161]](#cite_note-TCAC-168) They are [Toronto Pearson International Airport](/wiki/Toronto_Pearson_International_Airport "Toronto Pearson International Airport"), the [busiest airport in Canada](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_Canada "List of the busiest airports in Canada"),[[162]](#cite_note-busy-169) handling 44 million passengers in 2023[[163]](#cite_note-move-170) and [Ottawa Macdonald–Cartier International Airport](/wiki/Ottawa_Macdonald%E2%80%93Cartier_International_Airport "Ottawa Macdonald–Cartier International Airport"), Ontario's second largest airport, handling over 4 million passengers in 2023.[[163]](#cite_note-move-170) In addition to airports in Ottawa, and Toronto, the province also operates 11 other [airports of entry](/wiki/Port_of_entry#Airport_of_entry "Port of entry").[[164]](#cite_note-171)

A number of Ontario cities also have regional airports,[[165]](#cite_note-172) many of which have scheduled commuter flights from [Jazz Aviation](/wiki/Air_Canada_Jazz "Air Canada Jazz") or smaller airlines and charter companies – flights from mid-size cities such as Sudbury and Sault Ste. Marie, to larger airports in Toronto and Ottawa.[[166]](#cite_note-173)[[167]](#cite_note-174) [Bearskin Airlines](/wiki/Bearskin_Airlines "Bearskin Airlines") also runs flights along the northerly east–west route, connecting [North Bay](/wiki/North_Bay,_Ontario "North Bay, Ontario"), Sudbury, Sault Ste. Marie, and Thunder Bay directly.[[168]](#cite_note-175)

[Remote and isolated towns and settlements](/wiki/Remote_and_isolated_community "Remote and isolated community") in the northern areas of the province rely partly or entirely on [air service](/wiki/Airline "Airline") for travel, goods, and even [ambulance](/wiki/Air_ambulance "Air ambulance") services ([MEDIVAC](/wiki/Medical_evacuation "Medical evacuation")), since much of the far northern area of the province cannot be reached by road (or by year-round road) or rail.[[169]](#cite_note-176)

[Via Rail](/wiki/Via_Rail "Via Rail") operates the inter-regional passenger train service on the [Quebec City–Windsor Corridor](/wiki/Quebec_City%E2%80%93Windsor_Corridor_(Via_Rail) "Quebec City–Windsor Corridor (Via Rail)"),[[170]](#cite_note-177) along with *[The Canadian](/wiki/The_Canadian "The Canadian")*, a transcontinental rail service from Southern Ontario to [Vancouver](/wiki/Vancouver "Vancouver"),[[171]](#cite_note-178) and the [Sudbury–White River train](/wiki/Sudbury%E2%80%93White_River_train "Sudbury–White River train").[[172]](#cite_note-179) Additionally, [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") rail connects Ontario with key New York cities including [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York"), and [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City").[[173]](#cite_note-180) [Ontario Northland](/wiki/Ontario_Northland_Railway "Ontario Northland Railway") provides rail service to destinations as far north as [Moosonee](/wiki/Moosonee "Moosonee") near [James Bay](/wiki/James_Bay "James Bay"), connecting them with the south.[[174]](#cite_note-181) Regional [commuter rail](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") is limited to the provincially owned [GO Transit](/wiki/GO_Transit "GO Transit"), and serves a train-bus network spanning the Golden Horseshoe region, with [Union Station](/wiki/Union_Station_(Toronto) "Union Station (Toronto)") in Toronto serving as the [transport hub](/wiki/Transport_hub "Transport hub").[[175]](#cite_note-182)[[176]](#cite_note-183)

[Freight rail](/wiki/Rail_freight_transport "Rail freight transport") is dominated by the founding cross-country [Canadian National Railway](/wiki/Canadian_National_Railway "Canadian National Railway") and [Canadian Pacific Kansas City](/wiki/Canadian_Pacific_Kansas_City "Canadian Pacific Kansas City") (formerly CP Rail) companies.[[177]](#cite_note-184) As of 2021, there are 19,979 km of railways in operation.[[178]](#cite_note-185)

There are several city rail-transit systems in the Province. The [Toronto Transit Commission](/wiki/Toronto_Transit_Commission "Toronto Transit Commission") operates [subways](/wiki/Toronto_subway "Toronto subway"), as well as [streetcars](/wiki/Toronto_streetcar_system "Toronto streetcar system") (being one of the busiest streetcar systems in North America). [OC Transpo](/wiki/OC_Transpo "OC Transpo") operates a [light rail](/wiki/O-Train "O-Train") metro system in Ottawa.[[179]](#cite_note-186) In addition, Waterloo region operates a surface [light rail](/wiki/Light_rail "Light rail") system called [Ion](/wiki/Ion_rapid_transit "Ion rapid transit").[[180]](#cite_note-187) Construction on light rail lines, such as the [Hurontario LRT](/wiki/Hurontario_LRT "Hurontario LRT"), are also underway in the [Regional Municipality of Peel](/wiki/Regional_Municipality_of_Peel "Regional Municipality of Peel"), and are expected to be completed by late 2024.[[181]](#cite_note-188)[[182]](#cite_note-189)[[183]](#cite_note-190)

Main article: [Roads in Ontario](/wiki/Roads_in_Ontario "Roads in Ontario")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/Highway_400_Seguin.jpg/250px-Highway_400_Seguin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Highway_400_Seguin.jpg)

[Highway 400](/wiki/Ontario_Highway_400 "Ontario Highway 400") in [Seguin](/wiki/Seguin,_Ontario "Seguin, Ontario"). The roadway forms a part of the province's [400-series highways](/wiki/400-series_highway "400-series highway").

[400-series highways](/wiki/400-series_highways "400-series highways") make up the primary vehicular network in the south of province, and they connect at a number of points to border crossings to the United States, and [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), the busiest being the [Detroit–Windsor Tunnel](/wiki/Detroit%E2%80%93Windsor_Tunnel "Detroit–Windsor Tunnel") and [Ambassador Bridge](/wiki/Ambassador_Bridge "Ambassador Bridge") and the [Blue Water Bridge](/wiki/Blue_Water_Bridge "Blue Water Bridge") (via [Highway 402](/wiki/Ontario_Highway_402 "Ontario Highway 402")). Some of the primary highways along the southern route are [Highway 401](/wiki/Ontario_Highway_401 "Ontario Highway 401"), [Highway 417](/wiki/Ontario_Highway_417 "Ontario Highway 417"), and [Highway 400](/wiki/Ontario_Highway_400 "Ontario Highway 400"),[[184]](#cite_note-Ontario-191)[[185]](#cite_note-Highway-192) Highway 401 being the busiest highway in North America.[[186]](#cite_note-193) Other [provincial highways](/wiki/List_of_provincial_highways_in_Ontario "List of provincial highways in Ontario") and regional roads inter-connect the remainder of the province, and the Trans-Canada Highway connects the province to the rest of the country.[[187]](#cite_note-194)[[188]](#cite_note-195)

### Waterways

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Ontario&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Waterways")]

See also: [Boat building industry in Ontario](/wiki/Boat_building_industry_in_Ontario "Boat building industry in Ontario")

The Saint Lawrence Seaway, which extends across most of the southern portion of the province and connects to the Atlantic Ocean, is the primary water transportation route for cargo, particularly [iron ore](/wiki/Iron_ore "Iron ore") and grain.[[189]](#cite_note-196) In the past, the Great Lakes and St. Lawrence River were also a major passenger transportation route, including with the early 20th century growth of [Canada Steamship Lines](/wiki/Canada_Steamship_Lines "Canada Steamship Lines"), but in the 1930s, passenger traffic greatly declined,[[190]](#cite_note-197)[[191]](#cite_note-198) and though some cruise lines still exist,[[192]](#cite_note-199) as of 2022 less than 20% of traffic were non-cargo or passenger ships.[[193]](#cite_note-200) Ontario's three largest ports[[note 5]](#cite_note-201) are the [Port of Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton-Oshawa_Port_Authority "Hamilton-Oshawa Port Authority"), [Port of Thunder Bay](/wiki/Thunder_Bay_Port_Authority "Thunder Bay Port Authority") and the Port of Nanticoke.[[194]](#cite_note-202) Ontario's only saltwater port is located in the town of Moosonee on James Bay.[[195]](#cite_note-203)
